title: The Summer Movie Preview Game. Plus: David Cronenberg on ‘The Shrouds.’
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins run a new box-office-and-Metacritic prediction game across the 2025 summer slate, then Sean talks The Shrouds and Cronenberg's whole career with critic Adam Naiman and with David Cronenberg himself.

segments.heading: The new Academy rules
segments.note: watch-the-movies mandates, a casting Oscar, AI guidance

films.title: Sinners
films.note: This week's box-office sensation, floated as a ripe nominee in the new casting category; Sean uses it to walk through how the casting-directors branch will shortlist and bake-off the award.

films.title: The Verdict
films.note: Amanda's example of the courtroom 'disregard that testimony' trick (Paul Newman gets great testimony from a witness, then Milo O'Shea debunks it), cited while mocking the Academy's do-not-help/do-not-hinder AI guidance.

segments.heading: The Summer Movie Preview Game
segments.note: guess the domestic box office and Metacritic score; lowest combined miss wins

films.title: Thunderbolts*
films.note: Marvel's May 2 opener from the Beef team, selling Sebastian Stan and Florence Pugh rather than big stars. Box-office guesses $199M–$215M off a Black Widow comp; Metacritic 61–64 for 'polite' reviews.

films.title: Friendship
films.note: A24 comedy with Tim Robinson and Paul Rudd, out of Toronto, marketed with a corduroy 'male friendship' hat. Very funny in the room but I-Think-You-Should-Leave-coded and a tough theatrical sell; $12M–$22M, Metacritic 77–80.

films.title: Final Destination Bloodlines
films.note: Sixth in the series after a ~15-year gap, May 16. A reliable brand dropped into a crowded corridor; $42M–$46M, Metacritic 44–62. Sean and CR are planning a kill-rankings pod.

films.title: Lilo & Stitch
films.note: Disney live-action from Marcel the Shell's Dean Fleischer Camp, the big Memorial Day kids' tentpole. Nostalgia play but post–Snow White nerves; $180M–$218M, Metacritic 56 (both hosts).

films.title: Mission: Impossible - The Final Reckoning
films.note: The May 23 heavyweight and older-millennial draw. $202M–$220M, Metacritic 83 (both hosts). Sean hadn't seen it yet, prompting the running 'you have to make connections / send emails' bit.

films.title: Bring Her Back
films.note: The Philippou brothers' Talk to Me follow-up with Sally Hawkins, May 30. Less viral heat than Talk to Me; $23M–$48M, Metacritic 67. A $48M finish would rank among the biggest A24 releases ever.

films.title: Karate Kid: Legends
films.note: Roughly the sixth Karate Kid, May 30, with a built-in Cobra Kai base. The widest gap of the game: $66M vs. $113M (new-Ghostbusters comp); Metacritic 42–43.

films.title: The Phoenician Scheme
films.note: The Wes Anderson film Sean has already seen, May 30. $23M–$25M, Metacritic 75–78, roughly where Anderson has settled outside Grand Budapest, Moonrise Kingdom, and Isle of Dogs.

films.title: Ballerina
films.note: John Wick spinoff with Ana de Armas, June 6, reshot to fold Keanu in. Doubts about de Armas as a draw; $87M–$140M off A Quiet Place: Day One and Venom comps, Metacritic 42–57.

films.title: The Life of Chuck
films.note: Mike Flanagan's Stephen King novella adaptation, TIFF Audience Award, Neon, June 6. Sean saw it: 'the feeling of magic' plus a cheese factor; either a $70M crowd-pleaser or a small indie. $18M–$23M, Metacritic 62–66.

films.title: How to Train Your Dragon
films.note: Universal's live-action remake, June 13, from original co-director Dean DeBlois. A sturdy franchise with a sequel already announced; $168M–$250M, Metacritic 62–74.

films.title: Materialists
films.note: Celine Song's second feature with Dakota Johnson, Chris Evans, and Pedro Pascal, June 13. Big gap on faith in the A24 brand and Past Lives comps: $9M vs. $25M; Metacritic 73 (both hosts).

films.title: 28 Years Later
films.note: The Danny Boyle / Alex Garland reunion, June, pitched as high-class studio horror. $88M (both hosts) off a Civil War comp; Metacritic 77–83.

films.title: Elio
films.note: Original Pixar, June 20, punted out of 2024, with the studio visibly nervous. Low-end Pixar zone alongside The Good Dinosaur and Onward; $117M–$120M, Metacritic 72 (both hosts).

films.title: F1
films.note: Apple's Brad Pitt racing movie from Joseph Kosinski (Warner Bros. distributing), June 27, with obvious Top Gun: Maverick DNA. Debate over whether F1 travels in the US; $132M–$178M, Metacritic 61–74.

films.title: M3GAN 2.0
films.note: June 27, same day as F1. Both hosts kept it under $100M ($90M–$99M); Metacritic 64–68. Recreating the original's viral camp moment will be hard.

films.title: Jurassic World Rebirth
films.note: The seventh Jurassic, Gareth Edwards directing Scarlett Johansson and Jonathan Bailey, July 2. $366M–$384M, Metacritic 58–70. The springboard for Sean's rant that this money should fund original swings like Sinners.

films.title: Superman
films.note: James Gunn's DCU relaunch, July 11, 'probably the most important movie of the summer.' Huge gap ($257M vs. $410M) on the theory that the one-word title is the whole pitch (a Barbie comp); Metacritic 68 (both hosts).

films.title: Eddington
films.note: Ari Aster's A24 COVID-era New Mexico Western, July 18, counter-programming for starving cinephiles. $23M–$28M off a Beau Is Afraid box-office comp; Metacritic 67–91, Sean taking the big swing on critical acclaim.

films.title: Smurfs
films.note: The animated Smurfs movie with Rihanna as Smurfette. Sean smells a secret hit off a Garfield Movie comp: $90M–$114M, Metacritic 34–39.

films.title: I Know What You Did Last Summer
films.note: Legacy slasher sequel with Madelyn Cline, Freddie Prinze Jr. returning (Sarah Michelle Gellar's character stayed dead). Trap comp; $41M–$66M, Metacritic 43–54.

films.title: The Fantastic Four: First Steps
films.note: Pedro Pascal and a pregnant Sue Storm (Vanessa Kirby), July 25, with a well-liked retro-futurist trailer. $378M–$390M, Metacritic 56–67. Sean is betting on a Marvel bounce-back; Amanda isn't.

films.title: The Bad Guys 2
films.note: DreamWorks sequel with Sam Rockwell, August. 'Influencer-famous' but real playground currency among kids; $84M–$118M, Metacritic 54–61.

films.title: The Naked Gun
films.note: Liam Neeson comedy reboot that needs audiences to show up for a comedy. Good vibes among 40-something dudes at CinemaCon; $42M–$62M, Metacritic 53–59.

films.title: Together
films.note: The Dave Franco / Alison Brie body-horror movie out of Sundance with an awesome CinemaCon trailer. $16M–$36M, Metacritic 70–82, helped by a soft August.

films.title: Freakier Friday
films.note: Freaky Friday legacy sequel, August 8. Sean found the CinemaCon footage 'downright dreadful' and 'shot with a potato'; big gap of $72M (Mean Girls comp) vs. $117M, Metacritic 44–48.

films.title: Weapons
films.note: Zach Cregger's Barbarian follow-up, August 8, running the Longlegs mystery-marketing playbook. $53M–$72M, Metacritic 75–76.

films.title: Nobody 2
films.note: Bob Odenkirk sequel, August 15. $18M–$33M, Metacritic 56–58. A surprise the sequel exists at all, given the first's modest gross.

films.title: Honey Don't!
films.note: Ethan Coen's second solo feature, co-written with Tricia Cooke, reuniting with Margaret Qualley, a Drive-Away Dolls follow-up. $9M–$10M, Metacritic 56–72.

films.title: The Roses
films.note: The last movie of the summer: Jay Roach remaking The War of the Roses with Olivia Colman and Benedict Cumberbatch as the warring couple. $8M–$13M, Metacritic 62–67.

films.title: The Shrouds
films.note: Naiman's best film of 2025, seen at TIFF. Vincent Cassel plays Karsh, a grieving tech magnate (with Cronenberg's own hair) whose GraveTech shrouds livestream his dead wife's decomposing body; Diane Kruger plays three roles. A funny, arch grief film 'so close to not being good,' loaded with Cronenberg's checklist of doppelgangers, screens, conspiracy, and desire. Partly financed by Saint Laurent. Cassel is a 'drip god.'

films.title: The Brood
films.note: Where Naiman and Sean tell newcomers to start: Cronenberg's post-divorce, custody-battle 'evil kid' movie, his version of Kramer vs. Kramer, where family rage is literally externalized. Biological horror rather than supernatural.

films.title: Videodrome
films.note: 'A movie about the internet before the internet,' and a monument to Toronto pettiness: the Civic TV parody is Cronenberg's grudge against City TV founder Moses Znaimer, whose name gets a head blown off.

films.title: The Fly
films.note: The mid-'80s gore-fest that gave people a slightly wrong idea of him, but 'you don't get Jurassic Park Jeff Goldblum without Cronenberg Jeff Goldblum.' A best-Cronenberg contender for Sean alongside Crash and The Shrouds.

films.title: Crash
films.note: The Cannes provocation they had to invent an award for. Sean saw it at a COVID drive-in with a club's weekend playing over the fence.

films.title: A History of Violence
films.note: The Viggo Mortensen film that convinced people 'he's back' after Spider underperformed; Oscar-nominated for its script and, oddly, William Hurt instead of Mortensen or Maria Bello. Has post-Tarantino hitman comedy, including a great dropped gun.

films.title: Dead Ringers
films.note: Two hours nearly gore-free save the one dream scene where Geneviève Bujold bites the Jeremy Irons twins apart: 'a little goes a long way.' Sean finds it a better idea than a film and vows to revisit it.

films.title: Scanners
films.note: The one with 'punk rock fandom' thanks to the meme-famous exploding head and Michael Ironside's off-the-charts energy.

films.title: Crimes of the Future
films.note: The 'return to body-horror form' before The Shrouds, which Naiman thinks Neon mishandled by selling it like a William Castle you'll-throw-up gimmick.

films.title: The Substance
films.note: Coralie Fargeat's hit, constantly compared to Cronenberg. Naiman calls the comparison superficial (more manic than anything Cronenberg made) but notes his real inheritors (Ducournau, Fargeat) are largely women and queer filmmakers.

films.title: The Shrouds
films.note: Cronenberg, 82, on his most personal film: begun as a Netflix series (he wrote two episodes before it collapsed), reworked into a feature. Grief as a physical, bodily loss after his wife died in 2017; conspiracy as the mind's search for meaning; the GraveTech is 'totally feasible now.' He drives a Tesla like Karsh, has Bluetooth hearing aids and plastic lenses ('totally bionic'), and credits Howard Shore's score as 'another character' carrying the desolation Karsh won't show.

films.title: The Girl with the Needle
films.note: Cronenberg's answer to the show's closing question: Magnus von Horn's Danish film, 'beautiful and horrifying at the same time,' with fantastic cinematography.
